description: STAR/CARL behavioral interview scoring criteria with Amazon LP mapping.

criteria.0: Vague or missing context. No specific project, team, or timeframe. 'So there was this one time...' with no grounding.
criteria.1: Some context provided. Names a project or team but missing key details (when, what was at stake, who was involved).
criteria.2: Specific and grounded. Names the project, team, timeframe, stakes, and relevant constraints. Paints a clear picture.

situation.evaluation_notes: Look for: named projects, specific dates or timeframes, team size, business context, stakes. Deduct for: 'at my last company' with no specifics, hypothetical scenarios presented as real experience.

criteria.0: No clear action. Describes what happened to them, not what they did. All passive voice or 'we' statements.
criteria.1: Some actions described but mostly 'we did X'. Cannot distinguish individual contribution from team effort.
criteria.2: Clear 'I' statements with specific actions. Describes what they personally did, why, and the reasoning behind decisions.
criteria.3: Detailed 'I' actions with decision rationale, alternatives considered, and adaptation when initial approach didn't work. Clear ownership throughout.

action.evaluation_notes: I-vs-we ratio target: >60% 'I' statements for senior roles, >50% for mid. Track exact ratio. Look for: decision points, alternatives considered, adaptation. Deduct for: credit taking without specifics, vague 'managed' or 'led' without concrete actions.

criteria.0: No measurable outcome. 'It worked out' or 'it was a success' with no evidence.
criteria.1: Qualitative outcome described. 'The team was happy' or 'the project shipped' but no numbers.
criteria.2: Quantified outcome with metrics. 'Reduced MTTD from 45 min to 12 min' or 'resolved the incident in 3 hours with zero data loss'.
criteria.3: Quantified outcome with business impact AND personal learning. Connects the result to broader organizational impact. Articulates what they'd do differently.

result.evaluation_notes: Look for: specific numbers, percentages, time savings, cost impact, customer impact, before/after comparisons. Bonus for: learning stated without prompting, systemic impact beyond the immediate task.

criteria.0: Quality degrades significantly under follow-up probing. Contradicts earlier statements. Reveals memorized answer, not real experience.
criteria.1: Maintains consistency under probing. Answers follow-ups adequately but doesn't add new depth. Stable but not impressive.
criteria.2: Quality improves under probing. Adds detail, nuance, and reflection when pushed. Follow-up answers reveal deeper understanding than initial response.

depth_under_pressure.evaluation_notes: This is the key differentiator between real experience and prepared stories. Real experience gets richer under probing. Fabricated stories get thinner. Look for: new details emerging, consistent timeline, emotional authenticity, self-awareness.

interpretation.1-3: Weak behavioral response. Missing STAR elements, vague, or likely fabricated.
interpretation.4-5: Adequate. Has the structure but lacks depth, specificity, or clear individual contribution.
interpretation.6-7: Strong. Clear STAR structure with specific actions and measurable results. Shows real experience.
interpretation.8-9: Excellent. Deep, specific, reflective. Demonstrates leadership and learning. Holds up under pressure.
interpretation.10: Exceptional. All elements strong, reveals genuine character and growth. Would be a standout in any interview loop.

amazon_leadership_principles.description: Amazon's 16 Leadership Principles used in Bar Raiser interviews. Each behavioral question maps to 1-3 LPs.

principles.name: Customer Obsession
principles.description: Leaders start with the customer and work backwards.

principles.name: Ownership
principles.description: Leaders think long-term and don't sacrifice long-term value for short-term results. They act on behalf of the entire company.

principles.name: Invent and Simplify
principles.description: Leaders expect and require innovation and invention from their teams.

principles.name: Are Right, A Lot
principles.description: Leaders have strong judgment and good instincts. They seek diverse perspectives.

principles.name: Learn and Be Curious
principles.description: Leaders are never done learning and always seek to improve themselves.

principles.name: Hire and Develop the Best
principles.description: Leaders raise the performance bar with every hire and promotion.

principles.name: Insist on the Highest Standards
principles.description: Leaders have relentlessly high standards. They continually raise the bar.
interview_signals: raises bar for self and team

principles.name: Think Big
principles.description: Leaders create and communicate a bold direction that inspires results.

principles.name: Bias for Action
principles.description: Speed matters in business. Many decisions are reversible and do not need extensive study.

principles.name: Earn Trust
principles.description: Leaders listen attentively, speak candidly, and treat others respectfully.

principles.name: Dive Deep
principles.description: Leaders operate at all levels, stay connected to the details, and are skeptical when metrics and anecdote differ.

principles.name: Have Backbone; Disagree and Commit
principles.description: Leaders respectfully challenge decisions when they disagree, then commit wholly once a decision is made.

principles.name: Deliver Results
principles.description: Leaders focus on the key inputs for their business and deliver them with the right quality and in a timely fashion.

principles.name: Strive to be Earth's Best Employer
principles.description: Leaders work every day to create a safer, more productive, higher performing, more diverse, and more just work environment.

principles.name: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
principles.description: Leaders create more than they consume and always leave things better than how they found them.

custom_framework_template.description: Template for custom behavioral frameworks beyond Amazon LPs.
